name: ratatouille
instructions:
TWO VARIANTS — same DNA (french provincial vegetable-and-tomato dish), fundamentally different techniques. Variant A is the coarse lathera-style stew with sautéed sides. Variant B is the pureed-sauce-and-tianed-vegetables version (Thomas Keller / Disney movie ratatouille / confit byaldi).

=== VARIANT A — LATHERA-STYLE (COARSE STEW + SAUTÉED SIDES) ===

A coarse-chopped tomato-and-vegetable stew simmered in olive oil (borrowing the greek lathera braise technique) with the summer vegetables sautéed separately and served alongside or on top. Rustic, chunky, no puréeing.

INGREDIENTS:
- 2-3 ripe whole tomatoes, coarsely chopped (fresh, not canned)
- 2 bell peppers (green preferred — Julia Child's call), coarse chunks
- 1 fennel bulb, coarse chunks (fronds reserved for garnish if you want)
- 1-2 shallots, coarsely diced
- 4-6 cloves garlic, roughly chopped
- 1 zucchini, sliced into thick rounds or half-moons
- 1 summer squash (yellow crookneck or similar), sliced into thick rounds or half-moons (optional but recommended — pairs cleanly with zucchini and eggplant)
- 1 eggplant, sliced into thick rounds or half-moons
- fennel seeds (crushed in a mortar) + fennel powder
- tarragon
- black pepper, salt (see notes on salting lightly)
- generous olive oil throughout
- white rice for serving (see notes)

PROCEDURE:
1. LATHERA BASE: warm a generous amount of olive oil in a heavy pan. Add shallots, bell peppers, fennel bulb chunks, and garlic. Sauté briefly to soften. Add the coarsely-chopped fresh tomatoes and crushed fennel seeds. Simmer covered on low heat for 20-30 min until everything is silky and starting to break down — this is the lathera register (olive oil + vegetable braise). Season with tarragon, fennel powder, black pepper, and a light hand of salt near the end.
2. SAUTÉ THE SUMMER VEGETABLES SEPARATELY: in a second pan (do NOT crowd), shallow-fry the zucchini rounds and eggplant rounds in a generous amount of olive oil until each side is deeply golden and the surfaces have set. Work in batches. Pat dry after cooking to shed excess oil and water.
3. COMBINE / SERVE: spoon the lathera base into shallow bowls. Arrange the sautéed zucchini and eggplant on top or alongside. Serve over white rice.

=== VARIANT B — CONFIT BYALDI / DISNEY-STYLE (PUREED SAUCE + TIANED VEGGIES) ===

The fine-dining ratatouille — Thomas Keller's confit byaldi, the version made famous by the Pixar movie. Pureed piperade-style sauce on the bottom, paper-thin sliced summer vegetables arranged in overlapping rows on top, covered with a parchment cartouche and slow-roasted in the oven so the vegetables steam-brai in their own juices.

INGREDIENTS:
- 1 can diced tomatoes OR 1 can canned tomatoes (whole peeled) OR 3-4 fresh tomatoes
- tomato paste (a modest spoonful)
- 2 bell peppers, chopped
- 1 yellow onion or 2 shallots, chopped
- 4-6 cloves garlic
- 1 zucchini, sliced VERY THINLY into rounds (mandoline ideal)
- 1 eggplant, sliced VERY THINLY into rounds (matching zucchini)
- (optional) 1 summer squash, sliced very thinly
- (optional) 1 fresh tomato, sliced very thinly (to intersperse in the tiane)
- fennel seeds (crushed), fennel powder
- tarragon
- black pepper, salt
- olive oil
- (optional) white rice for serving, though variant B is often plated on its own

PROCEDURE:
1. PIPERADE BASE: sauté chopped onion/shallot, bell peppers, and garlic in olive oil until soft. Add tomatoes and tomato paste. Simmer 20-30 min until concentrated. Puree smooth (immersion blender in the pan, or transfer to a blender). Season with tarragon, fennel powder, crushed fennel seeds, salt, and black pepper.
2. SPREAD IN A BAKING DISH: pour the smooth piperade into a shallow oven-safe dish or wide oven-safe skillet, spreading it evenly across the bottom.
3. TIANE THE VEGETABLES: arrange thin slices of zucchini, eggplant (and summer squash and fresh tomato if using) in overlapping concentric rings or straight rows on top of the piperade. Drizzle with olive oil, sprinkle with salt and pepper.
4. COVER AND OVEN-STEAM: cut a piece of parchment paper to size, drape it over the vegetables as a cartouche. Bake at 275°F for about 2 hours — the vegetables steam-braise in their own juices without browning.
5. UNCOVER AND FINISH: remove the parchment and increase heat to 400°F for 15-20 min to lightly color the tops.
6. REARRANGE FOR PLATING: for the classic Keller/Pixar presentation, gently lift stacks of the cooked overlapping vegetables and re-arrange in a tighter concentric rosette on the plate over a smear of extra piperade. Serve.
notes:
TWO VARIANTS, ONE DNA: both are french provincial vegetable-and-tomato dishes with summer squash + eggplant + tomato + bell peppers + garlic as the ingredient core, and olive oil as the fat. What differs is the CUT and the ORDER — variant A is coarse-chopped chunks simmered/sautéed and served rustic; variant B is thinly-sliced-and-arranged, oven-steamed under a cartouche, then plated with intention. Neither is more authentic than the other — variant A is closer to the peasant-provençal roots of the dish; variant B is the michelin-era refinement.

=== VARIANT A NOTES ===

WHY LATHERA STYLE FITS: variant A borrows the greek lathera technique (coarse vegetable braise in generous olive oil) and applies it to the french vegetable set. The result is closer to fasolakia-lathera or a rustic italian caponata than to a plated ratatouille. Coarse chunks matter — the vegetables should keep their identity in each bite, not dissolve into a puree.

FRESH WHOLE TOMATOES ONLY IN VARIANT A: no canned, no paste. The coarse-chopped fresh tomato with its skin and seeds is doing structural work — the pieces need to hold shape and contribute pulp-and-juice character that canned/paste can't. Save the canned/paste for variant B's pureed base.

COARSE CHUNKS ACROSS THE BOARD: bell peppers, fennel bulb, shallots, garlic — all get a coarse chop, not a fine dice. This is lathera thinking: chunky vegetables braised in olive oil, keeping their identity through the cook.

SAUTÉ THE SUMMER VEGETABLES SEPARATELY AND DON'T CROWD THE PAN: same rule as the previous ratatouille versions. Shallow-fry zucchini, summer squash, and eggplant slices in a generous amount of olive oil, work in batches, get real color on them. Crowded pans steam instead of frying and give you pale soggy strips.

SUMMER SQUASH PAIRS WELL WITH ZUCCHINI AND EGGPLANT: yellow crookneck or similar summer squash reads slightly sweeter and firmer than zucchini and fills in a third texture register alongside zucchini's soft-vegetal and eggplant's meaty-collapse. The trio makes each variant more visually interesting (three colors on the plate) and each bite more textural.

FRIED ZUCCHINI + WHITE RICE STILL READ AS THE MAIN EVENT IN VARIANT A: the earlier bold claim (zucchini + rice are the meal, stew is a garnish, eggplant secondary) fits variant A cleanly. The lathera base is a rich seasoning medium for the fried zucchini and rice foundation; the eggplant supports.

=== VARIANT B NOTES ===

CONFIT BYALDI / DISNEY VERSION IS ITS OWN DISCIPLINE: variant B is fine-dining ratatouille — the Thomas Keller technique that showed up in the Pixar movie. Pureed piperade base (bell peppers + onion + tomato + garlic + fennel + tarragon, blended smooth) sits under paper-thin overlapping vegetable slices, all covered with a parchment cartouche and slow-baked at low heat. The vegetables steam-braise gently in their own juices without browning.

CANNED / PASTE / FRESH ALL WORK IN THE PIPERADE: because variant B's tomato base gets pureed, the tomato form is more forgiving. Canned tomatoes (diced or whole peeled), tomato paste, or fresh tomatoes all work — the blender flattens the differences. Canned + paste is often the most reliable choice for concentrated flavor.

SLICE THE SUMMER VEGETABLES PAPER-THIN (MANDOLINE): this is what makes the tiane work. Roughly 1/16 inch or thinner. Uniform thickness matters more than absolute thinness — a mandoline is the easiest way to get consistency.

COVER WITH A PARCHMENT CARTOUCHE — DON'T SKIP THIS: the cartouche traps steam over the vegetables while letting some moisture escape. Uncovered baking dries the vegetables out and browns them prematurely. Foil traps too much steam and turns the vegetables watery. Parchment (cut to shape, laid directly on the vegetables) is the right middle ground.

LOW AND SLOW, THEN COLOR: the ~2 hour bake at 275°F is the confit part — vegetables cook through gently. The final 15-20 min uncovered at 400°F is what gives them light color and finishes the tops.

REARRANGE FOR PRESENTATION: for the Keller/Pixar look, lift stacks of the baked overlapping slices with an offset spatula and rearrange them tightly on the plate over a smear of extra piperade. Optional for home cooking; standard for the fine-dining version.

=== SHARED / CROSS-VARIANT NOTES ===

NO VINEGAR IN EITHER VARIANT: don't add red wine vinegar, balsamic, or any acid beyond what tomatoes bring. Ratatouille sits in an olive-oil-and-tomato register that vinegar pushes toward italian caponata territory (a different, more sour-sweet dish).

SALT LIGHTLY — INCREMENTAL, NOT UP-FRONT: an easy failure mode is over-salting or brining the eggplant/zucchini before the cook. Common technique advice is to salt eggplant heavily to draw out bitter water; in this recipe that advice is a trap — the vegetables end up carrying salt into a base that's already seasoned, and the finished dish reads over-salted with no walk-back. Err on the side of too little salt on the vegetables. Season the tomato base incrementally as you build it, taste as you go.

DON'T OVERCROWD THE SAUTÉ PAN (VARIANT A) OR SLICE UNEVENLY (VARIANT B): both variants punish shortcuts in the vegetable prep. Variant A's sautéed strips need a hot generous-oil pan without crowding or they steam. Variant B's tianed slices need to be uniform thickness or the bake cooks them unevenly.

BELL PEPPER CHOICE — GREEN OVER RED/YELLOW/ORANGE (JULIA'S CALL): when using bell peppers, green's more vegetal/grassy character holds up against the tomato better than the sweetness of the colored varieties. Julia Child was explicit on this point.

BELL PEPPERS ARE OPTIONAL — FENNEL-BULB-ONLY VARIANT: the fennel-bulb + fennel-powder + fennel-seed path tastes more refined and is worth trying as a bell-pepper-free variant on either A or B.

GARLIC AND PARSLEY (USE JUDICIOUSLY): the dish sits on an axis between italian and french provincial, and the alliums + herbs are what pull it one way or the other.
- ITALIAN PULL: garlic + parsley + eggplant + tomato paste together pushes the dish into italian / eggplant parmigiana territory.
- FRENCH PULL: bell peppers + anaheim peppers + fennel bulb pull it back toward french provincial.
Match your alliums and herbs to which vegetables you're actually using:
- ZUCCHINI-LEANING PATH (zucchini + fresh tomatoes + peppers, minimal eggplant): garlic and parsley are welcome.
- EGGPLANT-LEANING PATH (eggplant + tomato paste, no peppers): skip the garlic. Lean on tarragon and extra shallot instead.

CAUTION AGAINST TOO MUCH TOMATO: the tomato element seasons and adorns the vegetables (variant A) or forms the base bed under them (variant B) — it's not the main event either way. Overloading with tomato pushes the dish tomato-forward and swamps the vegetable character. Modest quantities; if the sauce feels thin, reduce further rather than adding more.

SERVE WITH STEAMED WHITE RICE — BOTH VARIANTS: unusual for a western dish, but ratatouille pairs better with rice than with bread — bread soaks up the sauce wrong; rice catches and holds it. Both variants read well over steamed white rice: variant A pairs with rice most naturally (the lathera-style rustic serving is designed for it), and variant B's pureed piperade + tianed vegetables also sit nicely on top of a bed of rice even though the fine-dining plating traditionally goes without.
